facts.title: The Name 'Aztec'
facts.description: The people we call the Aztecs actually called themselves the 'Mexica' (pronounced meh-SHEE-kah). The term 'Aztec' was popularized by 19th-century historians to describe the people of Aztlan, their mythical homeland.

facts.title: A City on a Lake
facts.description: The Aztec capital, Tenochtitlan, was built on an island in the middle of Lake Texcoco. Today, modern-day Mexico City sits on top of the ruins of this ancient capital.

facts.title: Floating Gardens
facts.description: To create arable land, the Aztecs engineered 'chinampas'—artificial islands made of woven reeds and mud, allowing them to grow abundant crops directly on the lake.

facts.title: Mandatory Universal Schooling
facts.description: The Aztecs were one of the first societies in the world to mandate education for all children, regardless of gender or social status, though boys and girls attended different schools.

facts.title: The Triple Alliance
facts.description: The 'Aztec Empire' was not a single monolithic state but a 'Triple Alliance' formed in 1428 between three powerful city-states: Tenochtitlan, Texcoco, and Tlacopan.

facts.title: Nourishing the Sun
facts.description: The Aztecs practiced human sacrifice on a large scale because they believed the sun god, Huitzilopochtli, was in a constant battle with darkness and required human blood to rise each day.

source.name: History.com
source.reference: Why the Aztecs Practiced Human Sacrifice

facts.title: The Macuahuitl
facts.description: Aztec warriors wielded a deadly weapon called the macuahuitl, a wooden club embedded with razor-sharp obsidian blades capable of decapitating a horse in a single blow.

facts.title: Cacao Bean Currency
facts.description: Like the Maya, the Aztecs used cacao beans as currency. Small purchases, such as a rabbit or a piece of cloth, were priced in specific numbers of cacao beans.

facts.title: Obsessive Cleanliness
facts.description: Tenochtitlan was remarkably clean. The city employed thousands of street sweepers, and the emperor, Moctezuma II, bathed twice a day. The Spanish conquerors were astonished by the city's hygiene.

facts.title: Voluntary Slavery
facts.description: Slavery in Aztec society was not hereditary. Impoverished individuals or gamblers with severe debts could sell themselves into slavery, and slaves could buy back their freedom.

facts.title: The Templo Mayor
facts.description: The Templo Mayor was the central pyramid of Tenochtitlan, topped with two twin shrines: one dedicated to the god of war and the sun, and the other to the god of rain and agriculture.

facts.title: Advanced Aqueducts
facts.description: To supply the island capital with fresh water, the Aztecs built the Chapultepec aqueduct, a complex twin-pipe system. While one pipe was cleaned, water flowed through the other.

facts.title: Flower and Song
facts.description: Poetry was the highest art form in Aztec culture, referred to metaphorically as 'In xochitl, in cuicatl' (The flower and the song). Nezahualcoyotl, the ruler of Texcoco, was a famous philosopher-king and poet.

facts.title: The Game of Ullamaliztli
facts.description: The Aztecs played a fast-paced ballgame called Ullamaliztli using a solid rubber ball. Hitting the ball through a stone ring was so difficult that doing so instantly won the game.

facts.title: The Origin of Modern Words
facts.description: The Aztec language, Nahuatl, is still spoken today. English words like avocado, chocolate, chili, coyote, and tomato all derive from their Nahuatl origins.

facts.title: The Two Calendars
facts.description: The Aztecs utilized a 365-day agricultural calendar (Xiuhpohualli) and a 260-day sacred calendar (Tonalpohualli). The two synced up every 52 years, prompting the 'New Fire Ceremony.'

facts.title: The Spanish Conquest
facts.description: The Aztec Empire fell in 1521 to Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés, but the Spanish relied heavily on tens of thousands of indigenous allies, notably the Tlaxcalans, who hated Aztec rule.

facts.title: A Megacity
facts.description: At its peak, Tenochtitlan had an estimated population of over 200,000, making it significantly larger than most contemporary European cities, including London and Paris.

facts.title: Hairless Dogs
facts.description: The Aztecs bred the Xoloitzcuintli, a hairless breed of dog. They believed these dogs guided the souls of the dead through the underworld.

facts.title: Herbal Mastery
facts.description: Aztec physicians were highly skilled herbalists. The Badianus Manuscript (1552) details hundreds of medicinal plants they used, including passionflower for insomnia and muscle spasms.

source.name: National Institutes of Health (NIH)
source.reference: The Badianus Manuscript and Aztec Medicine

facts.title: The Tribute System
facts.description: The wealth of the empire relied heavily on a tribute system. Conquered provinces were forced to send regular payments of maize, jade, jaguar skins, feathers, and sacrificial captives.

facts.title: Eagle and Jaguar Warriors
facts.description: The Aztec military featured elite ranks known as Eagle and Jaguar warriors. To enter these prestigious orders, a warrior had to capture at least four enemies alive in battle.

facts.title: Base-20 Mathematics
facts.description: Like the Maya, the Aztecs used a vigesimal (base-20) number system. They indicated numbers using dots, flags, feathers, and incense pouches to represent multiples of 20, 400, and 8,000.

facts.title: The Flower Wars
facts.description: The Aztecs occasionally engaged in 'Xochiyaoyotl' or 'Flower Wars.' These were pre-arranged, ritualistic battles fought not for conquest, but explicitly to capture prisoners for sacrifice.

facts.title: The Dike of Nezahualcoyotl
facts.description: To prevent flooding and separate the salty waters of Lake Texcoco from the fresh spring waters used for farming, they constructed a massive 10-mile-long stone dike.

facts.title: Strict Alcohol Laws
facts.description: The consumption of 'pulque' (fermented agave sap) was heavily regulated. Drunkenness was a capital offense for young nobles, but elderly citizens over 70 were allowed to drink as much as they pleased.

source.name: World History Encyclopedia
source.reference: Food and Drink in the Aztec World

facts.title: The Pochteca
facts.description: The Pochteca were an exclusive guild of long-distance merchants. They acted not only as wealthy traders but also as spies for the emperor, gathering intelligence on foreign cities.

facts.title: Cochineal Red Dye
facts.description: The Aztecs produced an incredibly vibrant and colorfast red dye by crushing tiny cochineal insects that lived on prickly pear cacti. This dye later became one of Spain's most valuable exports.

source.name: Smithsonian Magazine
source.reference: The Bug That Had the World Seeing Red

facts.title: The Status of Mothers
facts.description: Childbirth was viewed as a literal battlefield. A woman who died in childbirth was given the same honored status as a male warrior who died in battle, becoming a spirit known as a Cihuateteo.

facts.title: The Board Game Patolli
facts.description: Patolli was a highly popular, fast-paced board game that involved moving pebbles across a cross-shaped mat. The Aztecs were enthusiastic gamblers and would sometimes bet their homes or their freedom on a game.
